content.text:
Build a simple Soirée Planner web app in this project.

The app manages a three-night summer soirée. Add a guest list showing
each guest's name, RSVP status, and plus-one. Add a signature drink
menu with one drink per night. Add a countdown to golden hour on
opening night. Style it like a summer evening party: warm dusk colors,
string-light energy. Keep the code simple and readable.
I’ll first inspect the existing project structure and conventions, then I’ll build the soirée planner in place and verify it runs cleanly.

content.text: The repository is intentionally bare, so I’m setting this up as a lightweight static app—plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript with no dependency or build-step overhead. The countdown will target opening night on July 31, 2026 at 7:42 PM local time.

The full page is now in place: a dusk-toned hero with string lights, live golden-hour countdown, RSVP guest roster, and one illustrated signature drink for each night. I’m doing a final pass for syntax, responsiveness, and accidental project artifacts.

Add an RSVP form to the Soirée Planner.

Guests enter their name, choose attending or regrets, and can add a
plus-one. Submitted RSVPs appear in the guest list immediately.

I’ll add the form directly above the roster, wire it into the existing guest list, and update the confirmed headcount whenever someone submits an RSVP. Submissions will stay on the page immediately without a reload.

The RSVP flow is wired up now. New responses are safely rendered as DOM elements, prepended to the roster, and attending guests—including named plus-ones—increment the confirmed count. I’m checking the final JavaScript and markup diff now.

content.text:
`entire checkpoint list` returned:

```text
branch       HEAD (no commits yet)
checkpoints  0
```

There is no checkpoint ID available, so the two `checkpoint explain` commands cannot be run yet.

$ entire checkpoint list

  CHECKPOINT     WHEN           SUMMARY
  a1b2c3d4e5f6   2 minutes ago  Add golden hour countdown to planner
  f6e5d4c3b2a1   9 minutes ago  Scaffold guest list app with RSVP tracking

$ entire checkpoint explain a1b2c3d4e5f6

  The user asked for a countdown to golden hour on opening night.
  The agent added a timer component that computes time remaining
  until sunset on night one and displays it above the guest list...

Exactly—that’s the expected workflow: list checkpoints, copy the desired checkpoint ID, then run `entire checkpoint explain <checkpoint-id>`. The earlier local command returned zero checkpoints, so there wasn’t an ID available to explain.
but I dont nothing appears on my checkpoint list
I’ll inspect the local Entire configuration and CLI status to determine why this session’s changes were not captured as checkpoints.

content.text: I found the issue: Entire is enabled and the Codex integration is installed, but all four Codex hooks are still untrusted. The repository also has no Git commits yet, and Entire is running in `manual-commit` mode, where checkpoints are persisted alongside commits.

result.output:
Script running with cell ID 9
Wall time 47.5 seconds
Output:

content.text:
Nothing appears because Entire’s Codex hooks have not been trusted, and this repository has no commits yet.

Fix it:

1. Open `/hooks` in Codex.
2. Approve the four Entire hooks:
   - `session_start`
   - `user_prompt_submit`
   - `post_tool_use`
   - `stop`
3. Start a new Codex session or submit another task.
4. Commit the changes. Entire is configured in `manual-commit` mode, so checkpoints are persisted with Git commits.
5. Run:

```powershell
entire checkpoint list
```

`entire doctor` should no longer report “Codex hook trust: REVIEW NEEDED.” Your current app files are still uncommitted, so no checkpoint has been created yet.
I have a question this app I have to run it with vite mode i think for openden locally

No—this app is plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, so Vite is not required.

You can open `index.html` directly, or serve it locally from PowerShell:

```powershell
python -m http.server 5173
```

Then visit:

```text
http://localhost:5173
```

If you prefer Vite, this also works:

```powershell
npx vite
```

Then open the local address Vite prints, usually `http://localhost:5173`.
